title: Antibiotic Therapy
subtitle: The nurse manages antibiotics — screening for allergy, drawing cultures before the first dose, teaching the patient to finish the full course, and watching for C. diff and superinfection.

patient.name: Antibiotics
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely administers and monitors antibiotic therapy.

nursesNote: The nurse safely administers antibiotics, which treat bacterial infections. Before the first dose the nurse screens for drug allergies, since the most serious risk is an allergic reaction up to anaphylaxis, and asks what reaction occurred. When ordered, cultures (such as blood, urine, or wound) are drawn before the first dose so the organism can be identified, though antibiotics for sepsis are not delayed waiting on cultures. The nurse teaches the patient to take the entire prescribed course even after feeling better, because stopping early promotes antibiotic resistance, and to take doses on time and evenly spaced to keep a steady blood level. Common adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset and the loss of normal flora, which can lead to a superinfection such as oral thrush or a vaginal yeast infection; new watery, foul-smelling diarrhea suggests Clostridioides difficile (C. diff) and requires stopping the drug if indicated and placing the patient on contact precautions with soap-and-water hand hygiene. Some antibiotics need level monitoring, such as vancomycin (peak and trough levels; nephrotoxic and ototoxic), which is infused slowly to avoid 'red man' flushing. The nurse checks food and interaction rules for each drug, watches for an allergic reaction, keeps epinephrine available, and reports rash, swelling, wheezing, or severe diarrhea.
providerOrders: Screen for drug allergies before the first dose
Draw ordered cultures before starting the antibiotic
Teach the patient to complete the full course and take doses on time
Monitor for C. diff diarrhea and superinfection
Monitor vancomycin peak and trough levels and infuse slowly

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about antibiotic therapy?

opts.text: Screen for drug allergies before the first dose
Draw ordered cultures before the first dose
The patient should finish the entire course
Stopping early once better is fine
New watery diarrhea may signal C. diff
Antibiotics carry a risk of allergic reaction
Sepsis antibiotics are not delayed for cultures
Loss of normal flora can cause a superinfection
Vancomycin should be infused rapidly
Vancomycin needs peak and trough monitoring
Doses are spaced evenly for a steady level
C. diff needs contact precautions and soap-and-water

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order starting a new IV antibiotic

opts.text: Verify the order and screen for allergies
Draw ordered cultures
Start the antibiotic at the correct rate
Monitor for an allergic reaction
Teach completing the course and report symptoms
Mild nausea with the antibiotic
Hives, lip swelling, and wheezing
A single soft stool
Profuse watery, foul-smelling diarrhea
Slight injection-site tenderness
Flushing and rash during rapid vancomycin

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Minutes into a new antibiotic, the patient has hives and wheezing
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop the antibiotic infusion
Assess the airway and breathing
Call for help and give epinephrine as ordered
Give oxygen and monitor vital signs
Document and update the allergy record
Finishing the full antibiotic course
Stopping the antibiotic once feeling better
Drawing cultures before the first dose
Infusing vancomycin rapidly to save time
Reporting new watery diarrhea
Sharing leftover antibiotics with family
Using soap and water for C. diff hand hygiene
Giving the drug despite a documented anaphylaxis
